content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : File                                                    Date  : July 22, 1979

                                                                   799-233b

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Sarah Saporta

Subject: Telephone conversation with John Ditter, Oakland City Councilmember


        Attached is a letter from Mr. Ditter, Oakland City Council.
On the 20th or so I called his office to ask him a few questions and
give him our response. He was in and he returned by call today.
        First, I asked him how many units were talking about as if it
was less than 4, he could rely on a "public generally" theory. He
said that one of the properties he was talking about had 24 units in
two buildings and the other one is unites.
        Second, I asked him why these units were not suitable for
conversion. He replied that studies of the problem lead him to believe
that only a small percentage of all apartments are suitable for conver-
sion. These suitable apartments are larger than average, at least
2 bedrooms or larger, they are in the best neighborhoods, they have
100% parking, and they are either new or have special amenities. For
example, he said, the apartment building that got all this started
was a new one overlooking a golf course in which all the apartments
were two bedroom or larger and in which the rents were from $300 to
$600 per month.

        In contrast, the 24-unit complex he owns has no parking and
small units and was built in 1917. The 18 unit one has units which
are probably large enough and has 100% parking, but it is also older,
c. 1930, and it is in a neighborhood where condo conversions are just
not happening. As he explained it, condo conversions are generally a
way to put the squeeze on the tenants and are therefore not undertaken
unless most of the tenants can reasonably be expected to afford to
buy the units.

SUTTER - D.2

I then asked him what effect the condo conversion ordinance
would have on the value of his property and the amount of rent he
could charge. He said that those questions were the key to the whole
issue and were hotly debated. Those who oppose condo conversions argue
that the total number of units are decreased and that the law of supply
and demand then drives up the amount of rent which can be charged for
any apartments left. Those who opposed the ordinance say that condo
conversions are bought by the people who paid rent for those same units
so that the decrease in the number of rental units is matched by
removing tenants from the rental market.

We discussed majority, and I mentioned that the absolute
standard established by the per was "significance" and that the $
amounts are to be considered, but are not completely determinative,
in deciding what is significant. I also told him that the more
problematical question in this case seemed to be whether any effect
was "foreseeable." I mentioned that the Thurner opinion talks in
terms of "substantial probability" and "likelihood." I told him that
our determination of these issues would rest on the same kind of
facts and arguments that are before him now, and that we have no
special expertise which allows us to foresee effects any better than
he is able to.

I asked him if he wanted written advice, and he said, "No."

79036

CITY HALL - 11TH FLOOR - 1 DR CARLTON B. GOODLETT PLACE - SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94102

John Sutter
XXXXXXX
Councilmember

July 13, 1979

State of California
State Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 'K' Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Sir or Madam:

Now pending before the City Council are proposals
to restrict or prohibit the conversion of existing apartment
buildings to condominiums. I have an ownership interest in
apartments which, in my opinion, are not suitable for conversion
to condominiums.

One of the arguments of the proponents is that
condominium conversions reduce the stock of rental units, creating
a scarcity and driving up rents. Proponents claim that there
is no evidence to support this argument and that it is incorrect.

Please advise as to whether, in your opinion, there
will be a conflict of interest for me to vote on this issue.

Does it make a difference whether I vote against
my supposed interest?

A formal written opinion of the Commission is not
requested and is not desired. A phone call response is adequate
to (415) 273-3206.

Sincerely yours,

JOHN SUTTER
Councilmember

JS/eg/vd

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember with ownership interests in apartment buildings has a conflict of interest in voting on proposed condominium conversion restrictions, and whether it matters if he votes against his supposed financial interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff discussed materiality standards with the councilmember, noting that 'significance' is the key test under regulations, and that the critical question is whether any financial effect on his properties is 'foreseeable' with 'substantial probability' or 'likelihood,' which depends on the same facts and arguments available to the councilmember himself.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of an internal FPPC memorandum summarizing a phone conversation rather than a formal written advice letter. The original incoming request letter from the councilmember is also included. No formal Q/C sections with verbatim text exist; the substance is conveyed through the memorandum narrative. The councilmember specifically requested a phone call response rather than written opinion, which was provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city councilmember with ownership interests in apartment buildings has a conflict of interest in voting on proposed condominium conversion restrictions, and whether it matters if he votes against his supposed financial interest.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC staff discussed materiality standards with the councilmember, noting that 'significance' is the key test under regulations, and that the critical question is whether any financial effect on his properties is 'foreseeable' with 'substantial probability' or 'likelihood,' which depends on the same facts and arguments available to the councilmember himself.

embedding.summary: This is a memorandum documenting FPPC staff's telephone conversation with Oakland City Councilmember John Gutter regarding potential conflict of interest issues arising from his ownership of apartment buildings and a pending city council vote on condominium conversion restrictions.
